title: AI in School: Know the Risks

modules.title: The Algorithm That Grades You
modules.sub: Uncovering how automated grading systems can go wrong
modules.description: You discover your school is using an AI system to grade written work, and your score doesn't seem right. You dig into how the algorithm makes decisions and explore real concepts like bias, opacity, and fairness in automated assessment. By the end, you identify at least two ways the system could disadvantage certain students.

modules.title: Privacy, Data, and Your Digital Trail
modules.sub: Mapping what schools collect and who really sees it
modules.description: You investigate what data the school's AI tools are quietly collecting — from keystrokes to browsing habits — and trace where that data goes. You examine real privacy risks, including data breaches, third-party sharing, and surveillance creep. You then decide which data practices cross a line and explain why using a structured privacy framework.

modules.title: When AI Advice Goes Wrong
modules.sub: Questioning AI guidance that shapes your future decisions
modules.description: The school's AI counselling tool steers a student away from a course they're qualified for — and you're asked to figure out what went wrong. You explore how over-reliance on AI recommendations can reinforce existing inequalities and erode student agency. You evaluate the scenario and make a judgment call about when AI advice should and shouldn't be trusted.

modules.title: Push Back or Accept? Your Call
modules.sub: Deciding how students can respond to AI systems they distrust
modules.description: Having investigated the risks across the school's AI rollout, you now choose how to respond — drafting a question for a teacher, writing a formal complaint, or outlining a student data-rights policy. You weigh the realistic options available to young people inside institutions and consider what collective action could look like. Your final output is a concrete, informed response that reflects your position on at least one AI risk you've uncovered.

synopsis: In this course you play a high schooler navigating a school that has quietly rolled out AI tools for grading, monitoring, and advising — and something starts to feel off. You'll investigate real risk categories like data privacy, algorithmic bias, and over-reliance, and decide what students should actually do about them.
rationale: Older students are subject to AI systems in their schools right now — from proctoring software to automated feedback — yet almost none have been given tools to critically evaluate or respond to those systems.
learning_outcome: Students can identify specific AI risks present in educational contexts, evaluate who holds power in those systems, and compose an informed response — whether a question, a complaint, or a policy proposal.
source_signals: The Risks of AI in Schools Outweigh the Benefits, Report Says
AI Teacher Assistants Are Useful but Can Pose Risks in Classroom, Report Finds - The 74
Teachers Want 'Guardrails and Guidance' on AI Use, Experts Tell Congress - Education Week
Tali Horowitz: The Key Issue with AI Chatbots and Teens
